citation.title: Organic solvent nanofiltration membranes with tunable selectivity using meta-aramid supports: Effects of PDMS coating, interfacial polymerization, and hybrid PDMS-interfacial polymerization process

content: A B S T R A C T Organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) membranes face significant challenges in maintaining chemical stability and separation performance, particularly in solvents with diverse polarities, viscosities, and molar volumes. To address these limitations, this study developed three types of OSN membranes using hydrophilic meta-ara┬¡midÔÇôbased supports, renowned for their exceptional solvent resistance. Distinct fabrication methods, including dip-coating, interfacial polymerization (IP), and a hybrid approach combining both techniques, were employed to tailor membrane properties for diverse separation applications. The dip-coated membrane with poly┬¡dimethylsiloxane (PDMS) exhibited high permeance in low-viscosity solvents, including hexane (36.2 LmÔü╗2hÔü╗1 barÔü╗1), ethyl acetate (30.3 LmÔü╗2hÔü╗1 barÔü╗1), acetone (21.6 LmÔü╗2hÔü╗1 barÔü╗1), and toluene (26.9 LmÔü╗2hÔü╗1 barÔü╗1), with an MWCO of 980 g molÔü╗1 in acetone, making it suitable for separating solutes in such systems. The IP-fabricated membrane, utilizing piperazine and trimesoyl chloride, demonstrated superior separation performance in high-polarity solvents, such as acetonitrile (2.2 LmÔü╗2hÔü╗1 barÔü╗1) and methanol (1.6 LmÔü╗2hÔü╗1 barÔü╗1), as well as in water (3.3 LmÔü╗2hÔü╗1 barÔü╗1), achieving an MWCO of 275 g molÔü╗1 in acetone.

Lastly, the hybrid membrane, combining PDMS coating with interfacial polymerization (IP), offered tunable MWCOs ranging from 624 to 248 g molÔü╗1, while maintaining high permeance in solvents such as acetone (1.5 LmÔü╗2hÔü╗1 barÔü╗1), acetonitrile (0.8 LmÔü╗2hÔü╗1 barÔü╗1), and ethyl acetate (0.7 LmÔü╗2hÔü╗1 barÔü╗1), demonstrating its versatility for a wide range of separation applications. This study provides critical insights into the design and optimization of meta-aramidÔÇôbased OSN membranes, highlighting their potential to enable precise and efficient separation in pharmaceutical, chemical, and industrial applications.

content: Journal of Membrane Science 721 (2025) 123824 processes is crucial for the separation and recovery of organic solvents as it enables molecular-level separation from organic mixtures with a fractional molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 200ÔÇô1000 Da [3]. For practical industrial application, OSN membranes should be chemically resistant to various organic solvents and require a high flux and rejection [3,4]. The support layer plays a crucial role in the performance and dura┬¡bility of TFC OSN membranes [5]. Common polymers used for support include polysulfone (PSF) [6], polyethersulfone (PES) [7], poly┬¡acrylonitrile (PAN) [8], polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) [9], and poly┬¡tetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) [10]. PSF and PES offer good mechanical properties and chemical stability but have limited resistance to aromatic hydrocarbons, ketones, esters, and ethers [6,11], and are prone to fouling due to their hydrophobic nature [7,12]. PAN, known for its thermal stability and chemical resistance, is used in ultrafiltration membranes but is unsuitable for NF or RO due to large pore sizes, requiring modifications such as plasma grafting [13]. PVDF and PTFE are highly resistant to chemicals and heat, making them suitable for harsh environments, but their inert nature and irregular pore structures complicate PA thin-film synthesis, necessitating complex processes like surface modification [14ÔÇô16].

citation.title: Organic solvent nanofiltration membranes with tunable selectivity using meta-aramid supports: Effects of PDMS coating, interfacial polymerization, and hybrid PDMS-interfacial polymerization process

content: their inert nature and irregular pore structures complicate PA thin-film synthesis, necessitating complex processes like surface modification [14ÔÇô16]. Additionally, PVDF and PTFE, as fluori┬¡nated polymers, impact the environment during the disposal process at the end of their lifecycle, releasing persistent and toxic compounds such as perand polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) [17ÔÇô19]. This raises sig┬¡nificant concerns the sustainability of membrane technologies relying on these materials and highlights the need to explore alternative materials. Meta-aramid fibers, characterized by rigid skeleton chains with amide groups connected to the benzene ring at the meta position and 3D hydrogen bonding, exhibit high rigidity, chemical stability, flame resistance, and organic solvent resistance, while also being inherently stable in both acidic and alkaline conditions [20ÔÇô23]. However, their solubility is limited due to intermolecular hydrogen bonds, requiring additives like LiCl or CaCl2 in solvents such as DMAc and DMSO to improve solubility [24]. In LiCl/DMAc, lithium ions bond to the DMAc carbonyl group, while chloride ions disrupt meta-aramid fiber hydrogen bonds, weakening their structure [25,26]. Residual salt removal and heat treatment can restore the fibers stability [24,27,28].

citation.title: Organic solvent nanofiltration membranes with tunable selectivity using meta-aramid supports: Effects of PDMS coating, interfacial polymerization, and hybrid PDMS-interfacial polymerization process

content: -aramid fiber hydrogen bonds, weakening their structure [25,26]. Residual salt removal and heat treatment can restore the fibers stability [24,27,28]. To achieve the specific application requirements for OSN mem┬¡branes, thin-film composite (TFC) structures are commonly utilized, consisting of a thin, dense selective layer (top layer) and a porous sup┬¡port layer (bottom layer) [29,30]. This layered configuration allows for independent optimization of the selective layer and the support, which in turn maximizes the membraneÔÇÖs overall performance by enabling each layer to be tailored to its specific function [31]. The selective layer is responsible for the actual filtration and can be created using various methods, including interfacial polymerization (IP) [32,33] or dip coating [34,35]. Interfacial polymerization (IP) is a widely used technique for forming polyamide (PA) selective layers [29,36]. This process involves a reaction between a multifunctional amine monomer, such as piperazine (PIP) or m-phenylenediamine (MPD), in an aqueous solution, and a multifunc┬¡tional acyl chloride, such as trimesoyl chloride (TMC), in an organic phase [29,37]. The PA layer that forms at the interface of these two phases exhibits hydrophilic properties due to the unreacted amine and acyl chloride moieties on the surface [38].

citation.title: Organic solvent nanofiltration membranes with tunable selectivity using meta-aramid supports: Effects of PDMS coating, interfacial polymerization, and hybrid PDMS-interfacial polymerization process

content: forms at the interface of these two phases exhibits hydrophilic properties due to the unreacted amine and acyl chloride moieties on the surface [38]. These unreacted functional groups can influence membrane fouling and solute-membrane in┬¡teractions, making them significant in the context of long-term mem┬¡brane performance [38]. Dip coating, on the other hand, is a simpler and widely applicable technique for forming a dense active layer. In this process, a porous support is immersed in a solution containing a prepolymer or monomer, such as polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) [39]. The PDMS forms a thin, dense layer on the membrane surface, imparting high hydrophobicity and excellent chemical resistance. PDMS-based membranes have been successfully applied in various separation processes, including gas separation [40], evaporation [41], and nanofiltration for organic solvent separation [42]. Their high permeability and chemical stability make them particularly suitable for harsh environments, where exposure to aggressive solvents or extreme conditions is common [43,44]. However, each of these fabrication techniques has its own advan┬¡tages and challenges. IP is highly effective in forming thin, selective layers, but the process requires careful control of reaction conditions to ensure consistent membrane properties [32,33].

content: e in forming thin, selective layers, but the process requires careful control of reaction conditions to ensure consistent membrane properties [32,33]. Dip coating offers a straightforward approach, but achieving uniformity and optimal per┬¡formance in the selective layer can be challenging, particularly when working with complex solvents or diverse solute mixtures [45]. More┬¡over, both methods are often subject to limitations related to membrane fouling and stability under long-term operational conditions, necessi┬¡tating the development of new surface modification strategies and hybrid approaches to improve performance [46]. As OSN technologies continue to advance, hybrid membranes that combine the advantages of both IP and dip coating techniques are becoming increasingly popular, as they offer the potential for highly customizable performance char┬¡acteristics, such as tunable molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) and enhanced solvent resistance [47ÔÇô51]. Meta-aramid has high hydrophilicity and excellent performance in aqueous solutions, making it a promising alternative to existing hydro┬¡philic supports [52]. Huang and Zhang developed a high-flux poly (m-phenylene isophthalamide) (PMIA) nanofiltration membrane using the phase-inversion method with polyvinylpyrrolidone and LiCl as ad┬¡ditives [53]. This membrane achieved a dye rejection of over 98 %, while maintaining a flux of approximately 20 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 and a low salt rejection of ca.

This membrane achieved a dye rejection of over 98 %, while maintaining a flux of approximately 20 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 and a low salt rejection of ca. 5.5 %, making it suitable for dye desalination and purification. Jin et al. developed a novel PMIA substrate-based PA TFC membrane for OSN using a continuous industrial-scale process. The membrane demonstrated high permeability and excellent solvent resis┬¡tance, outperforming current commercial OSN membranes [54]. Chen et al. fabricated a thermally stable PA TFC nanofiltration membrane using a PMIA substrate, exhibiting higher water flux and thermal sta┬¡bility compared to conventional PSF TFC membranes. Additionally, the PMIA TFC membrane maintained its rejection performance, while enhancing permeability at elevated temperatures [55]. To the best of our knowledge, composite membranes using a meta-aramid-based PDMS selective layer and OSN application of a meta-aramid-based composite membrane combining PDMS and IP in the selective layer are yet to be investigated. In this study, OSN TFC membranes were fabricated using a metaaramid support by employing three approaches: IP, PDMS coating, and a combination of both processes. The composite membrane incor┬¡porating PDMS in IP is expected to form a selective layer with a bilayer structure.

The physicochemical structure of the fabricated OSN com┬¡posite membranes was characterized in terms of the surface morphology, surface roughness, chemical composition, structural properties, and hydrophilicity. The optimal parameters of the PDMS and PIP content were investigated, and the separation performance of the fabricated membranes was evaluated through solvent permeation, dye rejection, and long-term filtration. This study presents a novel approach for developing high-performance separation solutions by combining PDMS and IP on meta-aramid support-based OSN membranes to achieve applicability and balanced permeance for diverse solvent systems, along with precise MWCO control. 2. Preparation of meta-aramid support A nonsolvent-induced phase separation method was used to obtain the meta-aramid membrane support. The casting solution was prepared by dissolving the meta-aramid fiber (16 wt%), LiCl (5.3 wt%), and PEG400 (5 wt%) in DMAc (73.7 wt%) at 80 ÔùªC overnight and degassing to remove air bubbles. A ternary phase diagram of the casting solution is shown in Fig. S1. Additionally, the time-dependent nonsolvent diffusion rates for the meta-aramid polymer solution and optical microscopy images of the polymer solution at different time are presented in Fig. S2.

Subsequently, the dope solution was cast on the PET non-woven sub┬¡strate using a casting knife to prepare the membrane (thickness: 250 ╬╝m), which was immediately immersed in water at 25 ÔùªC for phase inversion and stored in deionized (DI) water for 24 h to remove the solvents and additives. 2.3. Preparation of TFC membrane A selective PA layer was prepared via IP of PIP and TMC on the surface of the meta-aramid support. First, the meta-aramid support was immersed in an aqueous PIP solution for 1 min. Subsequently, the excess amine solution was removed from the support surface using an air knife. The support was then immersed in either a hexane solution containing TMC or both TMC and PDMS for 1 min to fabricate a selective PA layer. Lastly, the membrane was dried in an oven at 80 ÔùªC for 15 min. The membrane with PDMS was dried for 2 h to fabricate the TFC membrane. Table 1 summarizes the chemical compositions of the solutions. The prepared membranes were denoted as mA-IPx, whereas the membranes with PDMS were denoted as mA-IPxPDY, where X represents the PIP concentration the aqueous solution (wt%) and Y represents the PDMS concentration in the hexane solution (wt%). A dip-coating method was used to fabricate the TFC membranes.

Long-term stability test The long-term filtration of the membranes was measured using a dead-end filtration cell as follows. To prevent a concentration increase in the feed solution over time, the solution was replaced every 12 h. To ensure reliable performance, the membrane was re-compacted for 30 min following each feed replacement before resuming measurements. The separation equipment was operated under constant stirring at 250 rpm to minimize concentration gradient effects. The OSN performance (pure solvent filtration performance) was measured in the solvent after compacting the membrane for 30 min at 10 bar and 25 ÔùªC in a dead-end filtration cell. The dye concentration was measured using a UVÔÇôvis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Characterization of the meta-aramid support The meta-aramid support was manufactured using commercially available fibers, as shown in Fig. 3a, with a uniform diameter of approximately 10 ╬╝m. The surface and cross-section of the membranes were examined by SEM (Fig. 3b and c). A porous structure with a dense skin layer is observed on the membrane surface, and an asymmetric membrane with a finger-like substructure and thin skin layer is observed in the cross-section. The thermal properties of the meta-aramid fiber and meta-aramid support were investigated by TGA (Fig. 3d). The TGA curve indicates a slight weight loss at temperatures lower than 100 ÔùªC, attributed to the release of adsorbed moisture.

The meta-aramid fiber and support membrane exhibit similar weight losses at these temperature ranges, suggesting that the thermal stabilities of the support membranes did not deteriorate [27]. (Fig. S4), and the relationship between solvent and UF membrane per┬¡meance is mainly determined by the resistance encountered by solvent molecules when passing through the membrane pores. Solvent viscosity is a key factor that controls transport through the UF membrane, and it shows a linear relationship with solvent permeance (Fig. 3f). Assuming a viscous (convective) transport mechanism, solvents with lower viscosity face less resistance and therefore permeate the membrane more easily. As a result, hexane (0.29 cP) and acetone, with low viscosities, exhibit high permeances of 516.0 and 431.1 L m-2 h-1 bar-1, respectively. In contrast, ethanol (1.17 cP) and IPA (2.37 cP), with higher viscosities, show significantly lower permeances of 157.4 and 80.8 L m-2 h-1 bar-1, respectively. The solubility and swelling of the meta-aramid-based support were evaluated by immersing the membrane in ethanol, meth┬¡anol, IPA, acetonitrile, ethyl acetate, hexane, toluene, and acetone for two weeks. Conversely, N peaks of the PA groups did not appear in the spectra of mA-PD2 and mA-IP0.1PD2; instead, Si peaks (Si 2s and Si 2p) appeared. This indicates the shallow XPS probing depth of less than 10 nm that limits the analysis to the top surface layer, depicting that the membrane surface was mainly coated with Si [60,61]. Fig. 6b and 5c show the variations in the surface chemical structures of the mA support and mA-IP0.1, mA-IP0.1PD2, and mA-PD2 composite membranes obtained by FTIR spectroscopy. Similar spectra are observed for the mA support and TFC selective layer of the mA-IP0.1 membrane owing to the uniform functional groups of the same PA chemistry. The characteristic bands of aromatic PA, the CÔÇôÔÇôO stretching and stretching vibrations are observed at 1610 and 1650 cm-1 (amide I band), respectively. The NÔÇôH in-plane bending and CÔÇôN bending stretching vibrations are observed at 1540 and 1480 cm-1 (amide II band), respectively, in both the mA support and TFC membranes. The NÔÇôH stretching vibration of the amine group is observed at 3300 cm-1 [54,62]. With PDMS addition, the mA-PD2 and mA-IP0.1PD2 membranes demonstrate a peak at 2960 cm-1, which is characteristic of the CÔÇôH stretching vibrations of the CH3 groups in PDMS molecules.

Fig. 7c shows the separation performance of the mA-PD composite membranes with different PDMS concentrations for a 100 ppm RB/acetone solution. The interaction between the selective layer and RB is shown in Fig. S8. The negatively charged membrane interacts with negatively charged RB through electrostatic repulsion, significantly reducing dye adsorption. Membranes with PDMS coatings exhibit even lower dye adsorption due to their more negative zeta potentials. Visible color changes on the membrane surface before and after the test further confirm dye adsorption. As the PDMS content was increased from 1 to 8 wt%, the permeance decreased from 42.3 to 1.91 L mÔü╗2 hÔü╗1 barÔü╗1, whereas the rejection increased from 85.6 % to over 95 % at a PDMS content of 2 wt %, reaching a maximum of 99.8 % at 8 wt% PDMS. This phenomenon is attributed to the decreased membrane expansion owing to the inhibited chain mobility, which increased the transport resistance of the solvent and dye molecules [68]. Considering both the permeance and dye rejection, the optimal PDMS content of 2 wt% is selected for fabricating mA-IP_PD composite membranes. The MWCO values of the mA-PD composite membranes were tested with varying PDMS concentration using dye solutions of various mo┬¡lecular weights at a concentration of 100 ppm.

These results demonstrate the excellent dura┬¡bility and operational stability of these membranes. The separation performance of the PDMS-based meta-aramid mem┬¡branes discussed in this study was compared with that of various polysiloxane-based membranes (Table 2). The membranes coated with PDMS have a considerably higher permeance for hexane, toluene, and ethyl acetate and similar MWCO values compared to previously reported values [63,72,73], especially for the mA-PD2 membrane. Moreover, they show a higher permeance than the zeolite-filled membranes in PDMS [74]. Membranes with PDMS added to the IP, especially the mA-IP0.05PD2 membrane, have excellent separation performance with a MWCO of 382 g mol-1 compared to previously reported PDMS-based membranes. The incorporation of nanofillers, such as Mxene, may facilitate in improving the flux without greatly compromising the selectivity [75]. Recent advancements in OSN have led to active research on enhancing membrane performance and developing novel supports [76]. Conventional supports such as polypropylene or PTFE exhibit hydro┬¡phobic properties, requiring surface modification or the synthesis of intermediate layers to form a PA layer [77ÔÇô79]. In contrast, meta-aramid has hydrophilic properties, allowing the formation of a PA layer without the need for modification.
